summary: The F-One Trigger is a dedicated big air machine with explosive pop and hangtime. Its 5-strut design provides rigidity for powered megaloops and committed boosting.
best_for: Experienced riders chasing maximum height and megaloops

sources.summary: Big air enthusiasts appreciate the explosive lift and predictable looping characteristics, though some note it lacks versatility outside of powered riding.
Reviewer documents impressive hangtime and stable flight through megaloops, praising the 5-strut rigidity and bar pressure feedback.
Users compare favorably to the Duotone Rebel, noting more direct steering and better megaloop characteristics.

reviews.video_title: Big Air Kite Review 18 - F-One Trigger Brainchild
reviews.excerpt: and welcome once again to another plus-sized kite review. And this time around, it's my third go on a brainchild built kite, the F1 Trigger. If you want to get your hands on one of these beautiful looking send machines, you should definitely get onto the Mac kite boarding page or hit them up directl
reviews.verdict: g in the next few reviews. So, as always, please like, subscribe, drop a comment below if you liked the review or have any thoughts on this kite or any other kites I should be riding. And be sure to share this review in your local kite groups to help the channel grow and keep more reviews coming. Thanks as always for making it this far. reviews.full_transcript: Hello, and welcome once again to another plus-sized kite review. And this time around, it's my third go on a brainchild built kite, the F1 Trigger. If you want to get your hands on one of these beautiful looking send machines, you should definitely get onto the Mac kite boarding page or hit them up directly for a demo. And while you're at it, be sure to thank them for getting this demo model out to me for the review. If you're a fan of this channel and my reviews and want to help with a little of the cost of making them, you can also drop me a tip on my give a little page. The links in the description. You'll also notice that I'm using the Surfer app to measure my jumps and in particular their fun new surfy video overlay feature to help you show off your jump stats in your posts and social content. Now, with the housekeeping aside, you probably already know all about the exciting industry changing work this kites co-designer/manufacturer Ralph Groso is doing for the industry. But just in case you don't, I have included some links in the description to learn more about the technical side of the kite's design and construction, as I will just be focusing on my experiences riding it. For this review, as usual, I rode it over a few sessions, ranging from a lighter 20 to 25 knots all the way up to a solid half hour or so in a 30 to 35 knot mega looping fun session where I filmed loops like the one at the start of the video. I should point out though that on the very first session, due to a combination of my own hubris and not enough time spent on the kite getting dialed with it, I had a silly crash, as you can see here, where I injured my ribs and was off the water for a few weeks. Since coming back, I've had to be riding a seat harness, which I found a bit challenging to get used to. So, there will be a few less tricks than usual and likely a bit more off-balance riding here and there. That said, I was still in good enough state to get a great feel for the kite and to be able to compare it to what else is out there on the market right now. Now, starting off with the first impressions, as you would expect if you're familiar with Brainchild built kites, the overall feel is super light right from when you get it out of the bag to putting it down once inflated and of course once you get it in the air. Like with Lula kites, you certainly want to make sure you're not just leaving it laying around anywhere windy without something to weigh it down or it might take off on you. Immediately, the kite feels nice and responsive, but perhaps not quite as responsive as I was expecting for some reason. And I wasn't sure on this first session how much of it was the kite or for some reason the way the F1 bar reacted with it. After this first session, I tested the kite with both my Duo Tone Trust Bar and the Harlem Lead Bar and did prefer the feeling and general response of both of these options because they felt a bit more direct with the kite. To the F1 bar's defense, however, it was a smaller size bar than I'm used to, and I have been riding these other bars for months or years now, so it makes sense they would seem more familiar. Uh, so perhaps if I had a lot more time on the F1 bar, I would get in tune with it as well. The overall design of the bar is good and it's got a nice lighter weight to it as you'd expect from it being a 45x 38 size. The grip is solid and the sheet in andout motion is nice and smooth. So in general, it's a nice feeling bar. To nitpick, however, a little as I tend to do, one kind of annoying element is that although the high Ysplitter is adjustable, you need a little Allen key that comes with the bar to move it. So, of course, I didn't realize this and walked down the beach from the car park, set up all the gear with reasonable wind and a shitload of stoke to get on the water. And then after laying out the lines and realizing the w the splitter was in a lower position from whoever was using it last at Mac, uh, I had to rush back to the car park to find that silly little Allen key. Not exactly the end of the world, but it does seem a bit silly that it's not adjustable by hand, if you ask me. Even with the unfamiliarity of the bar on my first session, I immediately felt like the kite still flew incredibly easily relative to its performance. with a lighter to medium level of bar pressure when comparing it to other more modern kites of the last year or two. That would mean I'm guessing somewhere similar to say an Orbit Pro or a mediumweighted three strut ula kite like the Slingshot NXT, but not say as light as something like the Harlem Thrive, some of the lightest ulakites or the Reeden Hyper model. Right away, I felt so comfortable on the kite that my second jump was a loop around the 10 to 12 meter mark. And after only three or four loops, I threw this nice medium-sized boogie. It was only a few jumps later, however, that my confidence was my undoing, and I got a little too cocky with that back roll late back and just crank the bar in too early, which is certainly a no no with a super fast kite. and the kite shot around so fast that it threw me off my rotation and into a third too quickly and I wasn't prepared for it. So, I lost track of the kite and landed right on my ribs. Um, generally speaking with reviews, I tend to ride the kites for about a hour or so. Um, getting familiar with them and the conditions before I start adding rotations or board offs. So, this was really my own stupidity at play here. The buildup with a review usually goes along the lines of write it back and forth, boost to my right, boost to my left, throw some loops on my strong side, build up the loops on my strong side, throw a few loops on my weak side, and then after a little while start maybe adding rotations or board offs. The reason I felt so confident on the F1 right away was because it was a really nice balance of speed and responsiveness without being too erratic or too fast. Um, so despite only the 2530 minute session that uh ended in a trip to the emergency room, I was left pretty excited to get back on the kite for more action as soon as I healed up. This definitely felt like a kite with some solid performance that would handle a nice range of wind but with a fairly easy to fly behavior and I was dying to find out. So, after a few weeks off the water, I bought a new seat harness and I was back in action to complete the testing in sessions like the one you see here and enjoyed every session I spent on the kite with one notable half hour of some proper windy conditions where I really should have gone down to an 8 m but was still able to handle the nine respectively. So, let's get on to the categories, starting with boosting lift, power, and hang time. So, as you might have noticed in my reviews, I tend to say that super fast moving kites always have a great deal of potential, but can be tricky to evaluate completely accurately because it's hard for someone at my level to always hit the sweet spot in a really powered condition when riding them. This was the case with the likes of the Thrive, Gong, Force, or Pace Pro, even the Orbit Pro to some extent. Um, but this trigger is certainly getting near those kites for speed. However, I found it was a little bit more forgiving with a larger sweet spot than these kites, which translated to the feeling of a little bit more lift or a little bit more easily accessible lift coming off the water. The power and the hang time still feel a little shade below the top boosters like the Rebel or NXT, but that seems like a reasonably fair tradeoff for its looping and the confidenceinducing nature of riding it, which I'll get into shortly. Sadly, for board offs due to the injury, I wasn't really able to attempt any, but did simulate doing them by holding the board through a variety of scent jumps where I would hold it through the descent and the heli loops before landing and taking my hand off. And the continued lift through the descent and helies felt somewhere in line with the Rebel SLS or perhaps the Code V2. So perhaps just a touch behind the big Alula hangtime monsters like the Vortex Rebel or NXT. I also found the lower end power was quite respectable as well, particularly for a five struck kite, which I discovered in one session where I was able to get out and ride it when lighter, albeit a little bit less experienced riders were on their 10 and 11s. And it paid off choosing the smaller size from them because the wind ended up ramping up to around 20 from 20 to 23 knots up to around 25 to 30 knots and they were all struggling when I had a great time. So, good sign for the overall wind range of this kite. When it came to looping, well, there's probably no surprise that this is where the kite really shines the best. The general looping shape is similar to that of an orbit with touches of the Thrive in terms of its speed, but very consistent and easy to predict. It doesn't quite pivot on itself as fast and tightly as the Thrive or the Pace Pro, which I found in this size made the trigger heloops a little more predictable and easier for me. Although I still was overending the kite because of its speed when I was jumping, it wasn't quite as much or as frequently as I did with those other kites. Um, which was also nice for this super fast kite. You'll certainly see in my videos I usually do have that habit with fast smaller kites of overending it, particularly coming off the water and then often having the loops out the side of the window. But aside from my nasty crash in the first session, the kite always came around very nicely back into a safe place with very little piloting required. You'd probably heard me talk already about how some kites require a little bit more active piloting through and out of the loop so that when you go for the catch afterwards, the kite is nicely positioned for some lift on your descent. Well, the trigger had a beautiful habit of seeming to want to catch you with its second lift almost everywhere. You sent it, making it what I would say is one of the best options out there for riders looking to get into and progress with their kite loops, at least in the ideal loop progression sizes around 8 to 10 m. The kite is super fast, but a slightly fuller loop made it one of the more forgiving catchers I've tested when you get used to its speed. For more experienced loopers, because of how responsive it is, you are also able to pull your loops very early in a jump and ease off the bar with a bit more confidence to send the kite lower into the window through your loops, which will give you a better yank. But it's still a bit of a challenge to get it really low. So, if you're a rider that has no time for yanking your loops while you progress or work on tricks, any type of moderate to heavy pull on the bar at the top of your jumps will give you a super fast low pull, high and tight progression friendly loop that the top looping kites all provide these days. It'll just be harder uh for less experienced loopers to get the kite low if you really want a big powered mega loop style load. So, in looping summary, from starting with your very first loops and progressing to the more advanced looper, adding height and tricks, this kite is certainly one of the best options out there that I've tested. So, that now brings us to the confidence boosting nature of the kite. And as you'd expect from a kite that is fast, controllable, powerful, but not insanely powerful, that also loops in a predictable but friendly way. Well, it's all coming together beautifully for this kite as the proverbial jack of all big air trades, but perhaps not master of any specific one in terms of the other kites in the market. That equates to an overall excellent kite for progression and confidence boosting that also has great uh top end rider performance. I did find in the top end wind conditions that when things got a little bit gusty, however, the kite did have a little habit of flapping and visibly losing its cool from time to time. Certainly not as much as say the Thrive or the Gong kites did. And unlike the Gong in particular, it didn't actually impact your control of the kite. I believe in particular the wind flapping is actually intentionally done as part of the kite's unique design to allow for it to absorb more gusts. But I didn't ride it maxed out quite long enough to get used to this. So it was a little distracting and nerve-wracking at times. So that is a little bit of a confidence knock while you get used to it at least, especially as it gets super strong and gusty. One great aspect of the trigger was that it didn't get as respectively heavier on the bar as kites like the Vortex or NXT tend to as they power up a lot more. So despite it being visibly less stable, it didn't often feel like it was too hard to control at all. This top end for progressing riders might be one area where it would knock your confidence a little bit if you were just getting in the habit of pushing yourself in terms of max power in your kite, particularly in the smaller sizes. But the kite still handles it very well for such a light and fast mover. So, I can only really knock it slightly there. Still, such a fast kite is always going to be a struggle uh for progressing riders in smaller sizes. So, all in all, there aren't really too many areas to find fault in this brilliant kite for your average rider. And you'd have to say it's another very nice feather in the brain cap. It might not be the best thing out there for the prolevel competitor because it's not quite up there with the top boosting and hangtime potential of some of the top ulakites. At least if the big air judges are in the habit of rewarding huge sweeping low fully powered single loops and technical tricks. But for the bulk of your average senders like me out there looking to progress who are very loop hungry, it's certainly one of the best options on the market right now. It scores very well across the board. And at this stage, I'd have to assume it's going to be a very likely candidate for one of my top kites of 2025, but there are still quite a few doozies on the way, including a couple more Brainchild creations coming in the next few reviews. So, as always, please like, subscribe, drop a comment below if you liked the review or have any thoughts on this kite or any other kites I should be riding. And be sure to share this review in your local kite groups to help the channel grow and keep more reviews coming. Thanks as always for making it this far. If you want to stay up to date with where I am and what review is coming next, please follow me on my Instagram, Jason of Montreal. Until the next time. Goodbye.

structured_review.summary: The F-One Trigger is a fast, responsive big air kite that excels at kite loops, offering a rare combination of speed and forgiveness that makes it one of the best progression tools on the market. Its Brainchild construction keeps it impressively light, and its predictable loop shape inspires confidence from early sessions. The main caveat is that it sits just behind the top ulakite boosters for raw hangtime and can exhibit visible canopy flutter in heavy gusts.
pros: Exceptionally fast, predictable loop with a forgiving catch
Light construction makes it easy to fly relative to its performance
Good low-end power for a 5-strut kite, solid wind range
Confidence-inspiring for loop progression without being erratic
Bar pressure medium-light, doesn't load up heavily at top end
Sweet spot for lift more accessible than similarly fast kites
cons: Hangtime and raw boosting power slightly behind top ulakite rivals like Rebel or NXT
Canopy flutter and visual instability in heavy gusts can unsettle riders
Getting the kite low for fully powered megaloops is challenging for less experienced riders
F-One bar Y-splitter requires an Allen key to adjust — not hand-adjustable
structured_review.best_for: Loop-hungry riders from early kite-loop progression through advanced boosting who want speed and forgiveness in one package.
structured_review.not_for: Pro-level big air competitors chasing maximum single-loop hangtime or judges rewarding fully powered low sweeping megaloops.
structured_review.rec_blurb: The most forgiving fast kite on the market — loops catch you almost everywhere you send it.
